title: #47 + #46 — a dropped --cleanup flag, and a merge refusal that blames the wrong thing
action: Two worktree defects, same file, done together. Read CONTEXT.md D3 and D4 first. (#47, D3) `bee worktree merge --cleanup`: on the ALREADY_UP_TO_DATE path (`.bee/bin/lib/worktree-store.mjs`, the `if (!fs.existsSync(mergeHeadFile))` early return) the function returns before `attachCleanupOutcome` is ever called, so an accepted `--cleanup` flag is silently dropped — exit 0, and the CLI's no-op branch prints only 'nothing to merge; no commit was made', not even the cleanup hint the normal path emits. The blanket rule 'cleanup is strictly post-commit' is RIGHT for MERGE_CONFLICT and MERGE_VERIFY_RED, where work is not integrated — and WRONG here: ALREADY_UP_TO_DATE means the branch holds nothing main lacks, and the dirty-tree refusal earlier in the same function has ALREADY proved the worktree carries no uncommitted work. So cleanup is safe on this path and must run. `scripts/test_worktree_cli.mjs` currently PINS the opposite (asserting `!('cleanup' in parsed)` and that '--cleanup never runs on a no-op'). Per D8 that assertion encodes the defect: rewrite it to assert the new, correct behavior, with a comment naming why the old expectation was wrong and why the other two failure codes still refuse cleanup. (#46, D4) The worktree's directory, branch and `state.feature` are all derived from ONE slug at creation (`createFeatureWorktreeLocked`) — the reported 'three independent names' is wrong. What drifts is `state.feature`, which `handleStateSet` and `startFeature` rewrite freely with no worktree awareness at all. The paved road makes this near-inevitable: the agent is told to create the worktree at session-scout time, BEFORE exploring settles the feature's real name. The visible failure is that the merge check derives `expectedBranch` from the mutated field and refuses with WORKTREE_MERGE_BRANCH_MISMATCH blaming the BRANCH — which is correct and unchangeable — leaving the user at a dead end. FIX: persist the creation slug immutably when the worktree store is bootstrapped, and have the reader that resolves a worktree's feature prefer that immutable value over the mutable `state.feature`. Then the merge check is correct under any later rename. ALSO reword the refusal so that when the two disagree it names the drifted field as the likely cause, not the branch. Handle the pre-existing worktrees that have no immutable field yet: absence must degrade to today's behavior, never to a crash or a new refusal. RED-FIRST for both: a fixture worktree whose feature was renamed after creation must reproduce the wrong refusal before the fix, and a no-op merge with --cleanup must be shown leaving the worktree in place; quote both.

truths: red evidence for #47: a no-op merge with --cleanup shown leaving the worktree in place and saying nothing about the flag
red evidence for #46: a fixture worktree renamed after creation reproduces WORKTREE_MERGE_BRANCH_MISMATCH blaming the branch
green: --cleanup now runs on ALREADY_UP_TO_DATE, and still REFUSES on MERGE_CONFLICT and MERGE_VERIFY_RED — all three asserted
green: the renamed-feature fixture merges correctly via the immutable slug; a worktree lacking that field behaves exactly as today
the rewritten test assertion states why the old expectation was wrong
node scripts/run_verify.mjs exits 0 AND BEE_AGENT_NAME=w-test node scripts/run_verify.mjs exits 0
prohibitions: never make cleanup run on MERGE_CONFLICT or MERGE_VERIFY_RED — work is not integrated there
do not bypass or delete the pinned assertion; rewrite it with its reason (D8)
a worktree with no immutable slug must degrade to today's behavior, never crash or gain a new refusal
do NOT regenerate the four derived artifacts — the last cell owns them (D9)

trace.outcome: Two worktree defects. #47: an accepted --cleanup flag evaporated on the already-up-to-date path — exit 0, nothing removed, nothing said. It now runs there, because that code means main already has everything and the dirty-tree refusal upstream has already proved the worktree holds nothing uncommitted; the other two failure codes still refuse, since on those the work is not integrated and removing the worktree would destroy the only copy. The test that pinned the silent drop was rewritten with its reason, not bypassed. #46: reported as three independent names, actually one slug at creation with a mutable field drifting afterwards — and bee's own paved road causes it, telling the agent to create the worktree before exploring has settled the feature's name. The merge refusal then blamed the BRANCH, which is correct and unchangeable, leaving the user at a dead end. The creation slug is now recorded immutably at a gitignored runtime path (a tracked one would have made every worktree read dirty to merge's own pre-check) and preferred over the mutable field, with the refusal reworded to name what actually drifted and to say outright not to rename the branch. Worktrees predating the record behave exactly as before — proven both renamed and not.
trace.verification_evidence:
{
  "red_47": "Before the fix, a no-op merge with the flag: `bee worktree merge --id m1--wt--noop-cleanup --cleanup` -> exit 0, message 'is already up to date ... nothing to merge; no commit was made.' Worktree dir still on disk: true. Branch still present: true. Grant still registered: true. Result carries a cleanup field: FALSE. The user's flag was accepted and then evaporated, with exit 0 and not one word about it.",
  "red_46": "Before the fix, a worktree renamed after creation: `bee state set --feature settled-real-name` inside the worktree (exit 0), branch unchanged and correct at wt/scout-guess. Then `bee worktree merge` -> exit 1, WORKTREE_MERGE_BRANCH_MISMATCH: 'the worktree at ... is checked out to \"wt/scout-guess\", not its expected \"wt/settled-real-name\" branch — merge refuses to guess which branch to consume.' The branch it names is the correct, unchangeable one, so the message points the user at the only thing they must not fix. Work never reached main.",
  "reported_causes_were_wrong": "#46 was reported as three independently-chosen names. They are ONE slug at creation — directory, branch and state.feature all derive from it. What drifts is state.feature, rewritten freely afterwards by handlers that have no worktree awareness at all. And bee's own paved road makes the drift near-inevitable: it tells the agent to create the worktree at session-scout time, BEFORE exploring settles what the feature is actually called. #47's reported cause was correct.",
  "green_47": "attachCleanupOutcome now runs on the ALREADY_UP_TO_DATE return and the CLI shares its cleanup reporting across both ok outcomes. The same command now prints 'cleanup: worktree removed, branch deleted.', the directory is gone, the branch is deleted with git branch -d (not -D), and the grant is dropped from the main store. Five new assertions cover it, including that the no-op path carries NO 'cleaned up unchecked' warning — that warning means 'no verify command is recorded', which would be a lie on a path where verify was skipped only because nothing was merged.",
  "the_other_two_codes_still_refuse": "Asserted and passing, untouched: MERGE_CONFLICT + --cleanup attempts no cleanup and leaves the worktree in place; MERGE_VERIFY_RED + --cleanup likewise. They return before attachCleanupOutcome is reached. The distinction is the safety property: on those two paths the work is NOT integrated, so removing the worktree would destroy the only copy — while ALREADY_UP_TO_DATE means main already has everything, and the dirty-tree refusal upstream has already proved the worktree holds nothing uncommitted.",
  "green_46": "bootstrapWorktreeStore records the creation slug write-if-absent, and the feature resolver prefers it over the mutable field. A renamed worktree now merges and its work lands on main. The record lives under an already-gitignored runtime path, chosen deliberately: a tracked path would have made every worktree read dirty to merge's own git status pre-check — the same trap that forces the companion session file to be torn down first. That property is asserted directly, not assumed.",
  "backward_compatibility_proven": "Two assertions cover worktrees predating the record: one with no rename merges exactly as before, and one that WAS renamed still refuses exactly as it did before — same decision, same code, same first sentence, with only an explanatory clause appended. No new refusal, no crash. The writer never throws, and a null feature writes nothing (unit-tested).",
  "the_reworded_refusal": "When both fields are known and disagree, the message now names the drifted FIELD and says explicitly 'Do NOT rename the branch to match', offering the two real options. When the immutable record is absent it says the expected branch was derived from the MUTABLE state field and that the field is the likely drift. Either way the user is no longer sent at the branch.",
  "pinned_assertion_rewritten_not_bypassed": "The test that pinned the silent drop now asserts the corrected behavior and carries its reason inline: the old expectation 'treated the COMMIT as the safety property, when the real property is the worktree holds nothing that would be lost', plus why the other two codes still refuse. Nothing deleted, nothing worked around (D8).",
  "chain": "82 suites green both with and without the agent-name prefix, re-run independently by the orchestrator after it performed the whole feature's derived-artifact regeneration. Targeted suites at report time: test_worktree_cli 151/151, test_worktree_store 25/25, test_worktree_companion 10/10. Mirror byte-identity confirmed.",
  "workers_own_observations": "Two, both accurate and worth recording. The tree already carried the sibling cell's uncommitted work when this one started, so 'green at HEAD' did not mean green in the working tree — the worker verified its own red was not attributable to it by recomputing every manifest hash and by stashing to HEAD. And it noted the rendered plugin trees had been re-rendered mid-cell by something outside it, so any assumption that they were untouched no longer held.",
  "red_failure_evidence": "Before the fix, a no-op merge with the flag: `bee worktree merge --id m1--wt--noop-cleanup --cleanup` -> exit 0, message 'is already up to date ... nothing to merge; no commit was made.' Worktree dir still on disk: true. Branch still present: true. Grant still registered: true. Result carries a cleanup field: FALSE. The user's flag was accepted and then evaporated, with exit 0 and not one word about it.  ||  Before the fix, a worktree renamed after creation: `bee state set --feature settled-real-name` inside the worktree (exit 0), branch unchanged and correct at wt/scout-guess. Then `bee worktree merge` -> exit 1, WORKTREE_MERGE_BRANCH_MISMATCH: 'the worktree at ... is checked out to \"wt/scout-guess\", not its expected \"wt/settled-real-name\" branch — merge refuses to guess which branch to consume.' The branch it names is the correct, unchangeable one, so the message points the user at the only thing they must not fix. Work never reached main."
}
